title: Graphs & Networks
description: Graphs are the most tested data structure at FAANG. Mastering BFS, DFS, topological sort, Union-Find, and shortest path algorithms is non-negotiable for L4+ interviews.

sections.title: Graph Representation
sections.description: Choose your representation based on the problem. Adjacency list (dict of lists) is the default — O(V+E) space, fast neighbor iteration. Adjacency matrix is O(V²) space but O(1) edge lookup — only use for dense graphs or when edge weights are queried frequently.
sections.codeBlock:
from collections import defaultdict

# Adjacency list — most common in interviews
graph = defaultdict(list)
edges = [(0,1),(0,2),(1,3),(2,3),(3,4)]
for u, v in edges:
    graph[u].append(v)
    graph[v].append(u)  # remove for directed graph

# Weighted graph: store (neighbor, weight)
wgraph = defaultdict(list)
for u, v, w in [(0,1,5),(1,2,3),(0,2,10)]:
    wgraph[u].append((v, w))
    wgraph[v].append((u, w))

# Grid as implicit graph — no adjacency list needed
# 4-directional neighbors
def neighbors(r, c, rows, cols):
    for dr, dc in [(-1,0),(1,0),(0,-1),(0,1)]:
        nr, nc = r+dr, c+dc
        if 0 <= nr < rows and 0 <= nc < cols:
            yield nr, nc

sections.title: BFS & DFS — Traversal Templates
sections.description: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs. DFS explores depth-first — better for cycle detection, connected components, and topological sort. Always use iterative DFS to avoid recursion limit issues.

sections.title: Topological Sort — Kahn's Algorithm & DFS
sections.description:
Topological sort orders nodes of a DAG such that every edge (u→v) has u before v. Classic applications: build systems, course scheduling, dependency resolution.

Kahn's (BFS-based): Repeatedly remove nodes with in-degree 0. Detects cycles (if not all nodes processed, a cycle exists).
DFS-based: Post-order DFS, reverse the result.
sections.codeBlock:
from collections import defaultdict, deque

# Kahn's Algorithm — O(V + E)
def topological_sort_kahn(n, prerequisites):
    """
    n: number of nodes (0 to n-1)
    prerequisites: [(course, prereq), ...] — prereq must come before course
    """
    graph = defaultdict(list)
    in_degree = [0] * n
    for course, prereq in prerequisites:
        graph[prereq].append(course)
        in_degree[course] += 1

    queue = deque(i for i in range(n) if in_degree[i] == 0)
    order = []
    while queue:
        node = queue.popleft()
        order.append(node)
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            in_degree[neighbor] -= 1
            if in_degree[neighbor] == 0:
                queue.append(neighbor)

    return order if len(order) == n else []  # empty = cycle detected

# DFS-based topological sort
def topo_dfs(graph, n):
    WHITE, GRAY, BLACK = 0, 1, 2  # unvisited, in-progress, done
    color = [WHITE] * n
    result = []
    has_cycle = [False]

    def dfs(node):
        if has_cycle[0]: return
        color[node] = GRAY
        for neighbor in graph[node]:
            if color[neighbor] == GRAY:    # back edge = cycle
                has_cycle[0] = True; return
            if color[neighbor] == WHITE:
                dfs(neighbor)
        color[node] = BLACK
        result.append(node)  # post-order

    for i in range(n):
        if color[i] == WHITE: dfs(i)
    return [] if has_cycle[0] else result[::-1]

sections.title: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
sections.description:
DSU answers 'are these two nodes connected?' in near-O(1). Essential for: cycle detection in undirected graphs, Kruskal's MST, number of islands (alternative to BFS), social network components.

Two optimizations (both required in interviews):
• Path compression: During find, make all nodes point directly to root → flattens the tree
• Union by rank: Always attach smaller tree to larger → keeps tree flat
Combined: amortized O(α(N)) ≈ O(1) per operation (α = inverse Ackermann function).

sections.title: Shortest Paths — Dijkstra & Bellman-Ford
sections.description:
Dijkstra: O((V+E) log V) with a heap. Works only with non-negative edge weights. Greedy — once a node is settled (popped from heap), its distance is final.

Bellman-Ford: O(V×E). Works with negative edges. Can detect negative cycles (run V passes; if V-th pass still relaxes an edge, a negative cycle exists). Required for currency arbitrage, certain network routing problems.
sections.codeBlock:
import heapq
from collections import defaultdict
import math

# Dijkstra — O((V+E) log V)
def dijkstra(graph, src, n):
    dist = [math.inf] * n
    dist[src] = 0
    heap = [(0, src)]    # (distance, node)
    while heap:
        d, u = heapq.heappop(heap)
        if d > dist[u]: continue   # stale — lazy deletion
        for v, w in graph[u]:
            if dist[u] + w < dist[v]:
                dist[v] = dist[u] + w
                heapq.heappush(heap, (dist[v], v))
    return dist

# Bellman-Ford — O(V * E), handles negative weights
def bellman_ford(n, edges, src):
    """
    edges: [(u, v, weight), ...]
    Returns: dist list, or None if negative cycle exists
    """
    dist = [math.inf] * n
    dist[src] = 0
    for _ in range(n - 1):          # relax all edges n-1 times
        for u, v, w in edges:
            if dist[u] != math.inf and dist[u] + w < dist[v]:
                dist[v] = dist[u] + w
    # Check for negative cycles (n-th pass)
    for u, v, w in edges:
        if dist[u] != math.inf and dist[u] + w < dist[v]:
            return None  # negative cycle detected
    return dist

sections.title: Cycle Detection — Directed & Undirected
sections.description:
Cycle detection is a prerequisite for topological sort and appears frequently in dependency graph problems.

Undirected: Use DFS or Union-Find. In DFS, a back edge to a node other than the parent signals a cycle.
Directed: Must track three states (white/gray/black). A back edge to a GRAY node (currently in recursion stack) means a cycle. A back edge to BLACK (fully processed) is fine — it's just a cross edge.

sections.title: Staff+ — Distributed Graph Processing
sections.description:
Pregel / Bulk Synchronous Parallel: Google's Pregel (2010) processes graphs distributed across hundreds of machines. Computation proceeds in supersteps: each node receives messages from the previous superstep, updates its state, and sends messages to neighbors. All nodes compute in parallel, then synchronize at a barrier. Apache Giraph and Spark GraphX follow this model.

Graph partitioning: For distributed processing, the graph must be split across machines. Edge-cut partitioning (assign vertices to partitions, cross edges become network messages) is the default. Vertex-cut partitioning (duplicate vertices, assign edges) works better for power-law graphs (social networks).

PageRank at scale: Each iteration is a superstep. Vertex sends its rank / out-degree to neighbors. Convergence after ~30 iterations on billion-node graphs. At Staff+ level, you must consider straggler mitigation (slow workers) and fault tolerance (checkpoint after each superstep).

Graph databases: Neo4j (property graph, native traversal), JanusGraph (distributed, Cassandra/Scylla backend). At Google scale, custom graph systems (Pregel, Flume) are preferred over general-purpose graph DBs. For real-time graph queries, consider a distributed key-value store with adjacency list encoding.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• deque for Graph BFS — Efficient queue for BFS traversals
  • heapq for Dijkstra — Priority queue implementation for shortest path algorithms
  • NetworkX — Python package for graph creation, manipulation, and study of complex networks
  • LeetCode — Graph Problems — Curated graph interview problems (DFS, BFS, Topological, Dijkstra, Union-Find)
  • NeetCode — Graph Problems — Categorized FAANG graph problems with video solutions
  • Cracking the Coding Interview (6th ed.) — Chapter 4: trees and graphs — adjacency list patterns
  • William Fiset — Graph Theory Playlist — Comprehensive graph algorithm video series

details:
Grid = Implicit Graph: In grid problems (number of islands, walls and gates, word search), treat each cell as a graph node. No need to build an adjacency list — compute neighbors on the fly with directional offsets. Multi-source BFS from all sources simultaneously is a powerful pattern (e.g., '0-1 Matrix', 'Rotting Oranges').

Floyd-Warshall: O(V³) — all-pairs shortest paths. Use when V is small (V ≤ 500) and you need distances between all node pairs. Simple triple nested loop: dp[i][j] = min(dp[i][j], dp[i][k] + dp[k][j]) for all k.

Prim's vs Kruskal's MST: Both find Minimum Spanning Tree. Prim's uses a heap, grows from one node — O(E log V), better for dense graphs. Kruskal's sorts edges, uses DSU — O(E log E), better for sparse graphs. Kruskal's is easier to implement in interviews.
